title: Panforte
subtitle: Dense Sienese fruit and nut cake spiced with pepper and cinnamon
excerpt: Panforte is a flat, dense Italian confection from Siena: dried fruit, nuts, spices, and honey bound with a little flour and egg white, baked until set. It is not a cake in the sponge sense; it is closer to a thick slab of spiced fruit and nut brittle. Serves in thin wedges and keeps for weeks.
sourceNotes: Panforte (literally 'strong bread') is a medieval Sienese confection documented in Italian domestic cooking references from the thirteenth century. The modern form, using dried figs, nuts, spices, honey, and a little flour, appears in Italian domestic cookbooks of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries and is widely documented in English-language translations of Italian regional cooking guides. Ada Boni, Il Talismano della Felicità (1925), documents the Sienese panforte tradition. The basic technique and ingredients are public-domain and widely reproduced in English-language baking references.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 × 20 cm round panforte (serves 12 to 16 in thin wedges)
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Improves with keeping. Wrap in baking paper and foil and store at room temperature for up to 6 weeks. Best after at least 3 days.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven. Low and slow to set the honey-sugar syrup without burning the fruit.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf, 30 to 35 minutes until set but not coloured

glossaryTerms.definition: Panforte is done when it looks barely set at the centre and the edges have firmed. It will feel soft and almost liquid when you take it from the oven; it firms as it cools. The honey and sugar binder has a low set temperature — the panforte will be rigid when cool even though it seems underdone when hot. Do not bake until the surface looks firm; at that point the interior will be over-cooked and dry. The bake time is 30 to 35 minutes at 150°C fan; trust the timer.

content.text: Panforte is a confection, not a cake. The texture is dense, chewy, and slightly tacky, with the candied fruit and nuts clearly visible in each slice. It is sliced paper-thin and served with coffee or a glass of vin santo.

Preheat the oven to 150°C fan. Line the base and sides of a 20 cm round tin with baking paper and dust with {{icing-sugar}}. Combine the toasted {{blanched-almonds}}, {{hazelnuts}}, {{dried-apricots}}, {{glace-cherries}}, and {{mixed-peel}} in a large bowl. Sift in the {{plain-flour}}, {{cocoa-powder}}, {{mixed-spice}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Toss together.

Melt the {{honey}} and {{caster-sugar}} together in a small saucepan over medium heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Increase the heat and cook without stirring until the syrup reaches 116°C. Pour immediately over the fruit and nut mixture and stir quickly to coat everything evenly. The mixture will be very stiff.
Tip the mixture into the prepared tin and press firmly into an even layer with wet hands. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes. See the
: it will look barely set when hot. Leave to cool completely in the tin before turning out. Dust with more icing sugar and wrap in baking paper.
